findings.requirement: US-A AC: 'Отмена системного диалога пользователем возвращает на экран отчёта без сообщений, кнопки остаются кликабельными'
findings.detail: Tech-spec Flow A step 7 handles uri==null by re-enabling buttons and emitting no Toast — correct. However the in-flight guard flag is set BEFORE the SAF launcher fires (to block double-tap) and must be cleared on the cancel path. The spec describes the guard being cleared only on 'dialog callback returns', but never explicitly states the flag is always cleared — whether uri is null or not. If the flag is not cleared on cancel, a second tap after cancel will be silently rejected, violating the AC. The tech-spec does not call out the reset-on-cancel obligation for the in-flight flag anywhere in Flow A, CrashReportActivity description, or the double-tap risk row.

findings.requirement: US-B AC: 'Сохранить лог из баннера' uses buildExport(About) — but Flow B in tech-spec says buildExport(About) which includes live logcat. User-spec AC 'Содержимое экспортированного .txt' row says logcat placeholder only applies to CrashReport path. No issue there. BUT: bannerSave calls crashState.clear() which deletes BOTH crash.log and crash.log.dismissed. If writeTo() throws IOException the spec says keep crash.log — but crashState.clear() is called unconditionally on success only. The IOException path in the banner flow is not spelled out anywhere in tech-spec (Flow B step 4 only describes success, the risk row 'SAF write failure' covers CrashReportActivity and About, but not the banner explicitly). Missing error-handling spec for SAF IOException in banner save path.

US-C AC: fresh-install path (filesDir/logs/ does not exist) — 'экспорт из «О программе» работает без ошибок'. Tech-spec LogExportManagerTest coverage line mentions 'fresh-install path (no logs/ folder yet) → returns header + all-[empty] sections without IOException'. However LogExportManager.writeTo uses ContentResolver.openOutputStream(uri).use — if the URI is valid this is fine. The real gap is: LogcatReader spawns ProcessBuilder; on a fresh install the main process pid is valid and logcat will return normally. No issue here. But DeviceInfoCollector reads PackageInfo and ActivityManager — those are always available. The gap is actually adequately covered. Downgrading to partial-covered / no gap for fresh-install.
findings.note: Reassessed: fresh-install is covered by LogExportManagerTest. Severity minor as a reminder to confirm LogcatReader also handles the case where logs/ does not exist (it reads from process, not from filesystem, so it is fine).

findings.requirement: US-A risk: 'Bootstrap loop — сбой внутри самого обработчика краша не приводит к бесконечному циклу'. User-spec AC states this explicitly. Tech-spec covers it in the handler try/catch and in CrashHandlerTest ('internal failure → killer called exactly once, no re-entry'). However the Risks table entry for Bootstrap loop lists 'uses only strings added in Task 1 and the existing Theme.SanctumMachina' as a mitigation for CrashReportActivity startup failure — but does NOT address the case where the theme resource itself is missing or corrupt (e.g., the APK installed over an older APK with a different theme). The tech-spec declares the activity uses android:theme='@style/Theme.SanctumMachina' (which exists) but never verifies it is present in the `:crash` process resource context. This is a known category of bootstrap-loop trigger on Android that the spec omits.

US-A AC: 'strings.xml resource resolution in the crash process'. User-spec AC states all strings must be via strings.xml. CrashReportActivity runs in ':crash' — a separate process. Compose reads string resources via LocalContext. String resources are bundled in the APK and accessible from any process of the same app, so this is fine in principle. However if the Activity's setContent{} call fails due to a Compose internal error (e.g., missing theme attribute resolution), the outer try/catch in CrashHandler has already exited. CrashReportActivity has no outer try/catch of its own. A crash inside CrashReportActivity.onCreate will propagate to the ':crash' process's default handler — which is the system handler (no custom one installed there, correct per Decision 4). The user will see the system 'app stopped' dialog. This is the documented fallback. Tech-spec is silent about what the user experience is if CrashReportActivity itself crashes — the risk row mentions 'outer try/catch in CrashHandler' but that runs BEFORE CrashReportActivity is even launched. The risk row's mitigation is therefore incomplete: it prevents the main process from re-crashing, but does not address a crash inside CrashReportActivity itself.

AC 'Секция logcat содержит отфильтрованный по своему процессу и уровню ERROR вывод — при экспорте из «О программе»'. Logcat command in tech-spec is 'logcat -d -v threadtime --pid=<mypid> *:E'. The '*:E' filter sets the default level to ERROR for all tags, and --pid further filters by process. On some Android versions '--pid' flag is only supported from API 24+ (confirmed available on API 31 = our minSdk). No gap on API. However the 2-second timeout is a hard wall: on a device with a large logcat buffer, the subprocess may fill the pipe buffer and block before writing all output, causing the timeout to fire prematurely rather than on actual slowness. The tech-spec does not address pipe-buffer management (reading stdout concurrently while waiting for process exit). This is a known Java ProcessBuilder hazard. LogcatReaderTest stubs CommandRunner so it never exercises real pipe behavior.

Second process spawn failure risk (user-specified check item). If startActivity(Intent(ctx, CrashReportActivity)) silently fails (e.g., ActivityManager refuses to start it because the manifest entry is missing or the process limit is hit), the main process still calls Process.killProcess. The user will see the system crash dialog but no CrashReportActivity. The tech-spec does NOT document this failure mode in the Risks table. It is partially mitigated by the assembleDebug structural grep check (Task 11), but only catches manifest absence, not runtime spawn failures.
Killer interface seam (CrashHandler)
findings.detail: CrashHandler takes a Killer interface seam 'to allow unit-testing without actually killing the JVM'. In a Robolectric/pure-JVM environment, Process.killProcess() is a no-op (Robolectric stubs it) and does not kill the test JVM. The seam is therefore unnecessary for testability — Robolectric already handles this. Adding a Killer interface introduces an extra abstraction with a single implementation (RealKiller calling Process.killProcess) and one test fake. YAGNI: the justification given in the tech-spec does not hold for the declared test environment.

findings.requirement: CommandRunner interface seam (LogcatReader)
findings.detail: LogcatReader uses a CommandRunner interface to allow ProcessBuilder stubbing in tests. Unlike Killer, this seam IS justified: ProcessBuilder actually spawns a child process in Robolectric/pure-JVM unit tests and will attempt to run 'logcat' (which does not exist on the CI host), producing a real IOException or hanging. CommandRunner allows deterministic fake output. This abstraction is justified by the testing strategy. No finding.
findings.note: Borderline — CommandRunner is justified. Severity minor only to flag for reviewer awareness. Not a true overengineering finding.

findings.requirement: ExportSource enum (LogExportManager)
findings.detail: ExportSource has exactly two values: About and CrashReport. The only behavioral difference is whether the logcat section is live output or a placeholder string. This could be expressed as a boolean parameter 'includeLogcat: Boolean' with zero abstraction overhead. An enum implies the set of sources may grow (Phase 5 Settings screen export is mentioned in user-spec 'Зачем'), which is a future-requirement justification — YAGNI for the current phase. The tech-spec User-Spec Deviations section says 'no feature is added that user-spec does not ask for' yet the enum design anticipates Phase 5 extensibility. This is a minor YAGNI abstraction; the enum is harmless but technically premature.

findings.requirement: TapCounter as a separate class
findings.detail: TapCounter is a pure-JVM 7-tap detector with no Android deps, injected with 'nowNanos: () -> Long'. It is used in exactly one place (AboutScreen version Text). The abstraction into a standalone class is lightweight and the testability benefit is real (TapCounterTest uses the clock injection directly). For a class of ~30 lines this is proportionate. Not a finding.
findings.note: Not flagged as overengineering — justified by testability and clean separation.

findings.requirement: AC 'Повторное нажатие «Сохранить лог» до открытия системного диалога не запускает второй диалог' (double-tap guard). Tech-spec covers in-flight guard flag in CrashReportActivity. For ModelManagerScreen (banner 'Сохранить лог'), the tech-spec Risks row mentions 'In-flight guard flag in both CrashReportActivity and RestartCrashBanner'. However the Architecture section for RestartCrashBanner only describes it as a composable 'Card { Row { Icon, Text, TextButton, IconButton } }' — no mention of the in-flight guard state being part of the composable or its caller. The double-tap guard for the banner path is mentioned in Risks but not specified in the Architecture or Flow B description. Implementation may miss it.
Constraint: 'Приватность — фильтрация не применяется на Phase 2.5, приоритет у полноты диагностики'
findings.detail: Tech-spec Security Audit task (Task 9) mentions 'A08 integrity: SELinux-context / hardware-identifier leakage from logcat, full stacktraces in crash.log'. This is correct for the audit. However the tech-spec itself (not the audit task) contains no explicit acknowledgement of the privacy constraint in its Decisions or Data Models sections. The decision to include full logcat and full stacktraces without filtering is a deliberate privacy trade-off that warrants a Decision entry (or at minimum an explicit Deviation acknowledgement). It is only partially surfaced via the Security Audit task scope.

findings.requirement: AC 'errors.log.1 включается только если файл есть' — the exported .txt Data Model in tech-spec shows the section as always present with '<content, or skipped entirely if file does not exist>'. This matches the AC. LogExportManagerTest explicitly covers 'errors.log.1 absent → section omitted'. Covered. No finding.

Testing depth for size M: six unit test classes, no integration, no E2E. User-spec 'Тестирование' section justifies the absence of integration/E2E tests via androidTest infrastructure gap. Tech-spec Testing Strategy echoes this justification and cites architecture.md § Testing. The justification is documented and self-consistent. However for size M, the unit test suite has a coverage gap: there is no test for LogExportManager.writeTo() — the method that calls ContentResolver.openOutputStream(). The test suite covers buildExport() extensively but writeTo() on an IOException path is only described in Flow A prose and the risk row. No unit test class is specified that stubs ContentResolver and asserts that an IOException from openOutputStream propagates correctly (keeps crash.log, surfaces error). This is a missing test for a critical system boundary.

AC 'CrashHandler time budget' — CrashHandlerTest includes 'handler body executes under hard time budget (smoke assertion on elapsed nanos)'. This is mentioned but no specific budget is stated in the tech-spec (Decision 8 mentions '~10 ms to dodge ANR-cascade' only in context of why logcat is NOT collected in the handler). The test is described as a 'smoke assertion' with no concrete threshold defined. Without a defined threshold the test is unverifiable and may be vacuous.
Solution section depth
findings.detail: The Solution section provides real architectural substance: it names specific components (CrashHandler, LogExportManager, CrashState), specifies the inter-process boundary, the filesystem-only state model, the in-memory string assembly on Dispatchers.IO, and the per-source logcat behavior. This goes meaningfully beyond the user-spec. No shallow_solution finding.
findings.note: Solution is substantive. Not a finding.

findings.requirement: US-B: 'Новый краш сбрасывает скрытость — новый handler удалит .dismissed вместе с перезаписью crash.log'. Tech-spec Flow A step 3a: '(a) deletes crash.log.dismissed'. The CrashHandlerTest covers 'pre-existing .dismissed deleted during write'. This is covered. No gap.

Constraint: 'Никаких новых внешних зависимостей'. Tech-spec Dependencies section states 'New packages: None' and lists only existing dependencies. Acceptance Criteria includes 'No new external dependencies in gradle/libs.versions.toml or app/build.gradle.kts'. Covered.

US-C: logcat command is 'logcat -d -v threadtime --pid=<ownpid> *:E'. Tech-spec Architecture LogcatReader description says 'ProcessBuilder("logcat", "-d", "-v", "threadtime", "--pid=${Process.myPid()}", "*:E")'. Matches user-spec exactly. Covered.

requirement_coverage_detail.US-B_banner_save_SAF: partial — SAF IOException path in banner not specified (F-02)
requirement_coverage_detail.AC_bootstrap_loop_prevention: partial — CrashReportActivity own crash not addressed (F-05)
requirement_coverage_detail.AC_cancel_returns_to_screen: partial — in-flight flag reset on cancel not explicit (F-01)
requirement_coverage_detail.AC_IOException_error_toast: covered for CrashReportActivity; missing for banner (F-02)

summary: 25/28 requirements covered; 2 partial; 1 gap (SAF IOException path in banner flow, F-02). 5 critical/major gaps found. Key issues: (1) in-flight guard reset on SAF cancel not explicitly specified for CrashReportActivity (F-01, major); (2) banner SAF IOException path has no error-handling spec (F-02, major); (3) CrashReportActivity own crash is not addressed in the bootstrap-loop risk mitigation (F-05, major); (4) ProcessBuilder pipe-buffer hazard in LogcatReader is unaddressed and untestable via the CommandRunner stub (F-06, major); (5) double-tap guard for banner is in Risks but absent from Architecture/Flow B (F-12, major); (6) writeTo() IOException path has no unit test coverage (F-15, major); (7) Killer interface seam is unjustified in a Robolectric environment — YAGNI abstraction (F-08, major). Tech-spec status is draft, which is consistent with these open points. No scope creep detected. Solution section is substantive. User-Spec Deviations section is consistent with Decisions. Structural integrity is sound — all decisions are in the Decisions section.
